questions.question: Explain the structure of an Ethereum transaction. Describe each field and its purpose.
keyPoints: Nonce: Sequential counter of transactions from the sender; prevents replay attacks and ensures ordering.
Gas Price (legacy) / Max Fee Per Gas (EIP-1559): Amount in wei the sender is willing to pay per unit of gas.
Gas Limit: Maximum amount of gas the transaction can consume.
To: Recipient address (EOA for transfers, contract address for interactions, null for deployment).
Value: Amount of ETH to transfer in wei.
Data: Empty for simple transfers; ABI-encoded function call for contract interaction; bytecode for deployment.
v, r, s: ECDSA signature components proving the sender authorized the transaction.

questions.question: Differentiate between the three types of Ethereum transactions with examples.
keyPoints: Simple ETH transfer: to=EOA address, data=empty, value=ETH amount. Example: Sending 1 ETH to a friend.
Contract deployment: to=null, data=compiled bytecode. Creates new contract at deterministic address.
Contract interaction: to=contract address, data=ABI-encoded function call. Example: Calling transfer() on an ERC-20 token.
All three share the same base structure (nonce, gas, signature) but differ in to and data fields.

questions.question: Explain the EIP-1559 fee mechanism. How does it differ from the legacy gas pricing model?
keyPoints: Legacy: Transaction Fee = Gas Used x Gas Price. Gas price set entirely by user; first-price auction.
EIP-1559: Fee = Gas Used x (Base Fee + Priority Fee). Base fee is algorithmically determined and burned.
Priority fee (tip) goes to validator; Max Fee Per Gas sets the ceiling; surplus is refunded.
Benefits: More predictable fees, better UX, deflationary pressure on ETH from burning base fee.

questions.question: Describe the complete lifecycle of an Ethereum transaction from creation to finality.
keyPoints: Creation: User constructs transaction fields and signs with private key using ECDSA.
Broadcasting: Signed transaction is broadcast to the Ethereum peer-to-peer network.
Mempool: Transaction waits in the transaction pool; validators select highest-fee transactions.
Execution: Validator includes transaction in block; EVM executes it and updates world state.
Finality: Under PoS, block is finalized after 2 epochs (~12.8 minutes); transaction becomes irreversible.
